# rpi-ipad-usb
RPI iPad USB [AI-GEN TEST]

> [!NOTE]
> This code is COMPLETELY AI-Generated and is mainly for a proof of concept/testing purposes only.

### Readme generated by AI
# 🍓 RPi Link v3 — iPad USB Dashboard

Full iPad remote desktop over a single USB-C cable. **Works on Pi 4 and Pi 5.**

```
iPad Safari ←──── USB-C cable ────→ Raspberry Pi 4 or 5
            live video · audio · touch · files
```

---

## What's new in v3

| | |
|---|---|
| **Pi 5 support** | Uses `libcomposite`/ConfigFS instead of legacy `g_ether` — the only method that works on Pi 5 Bookworm |
| **Uninstall script** | `sudo bash uninstall.sh` cleanly removes everything |
| **Gadget status** | Stats tab shows whether the USB gadget is bound correctly |
| **Pi model display** | Shows exact Pi model in Stats |
| **Better thermal** | Reads Pi 5's correct thermal zone |
| **Cleaner NM integration** | Uses NetworkManager profiles (required on Bookworm) instead of `/etc/network/interfaces` |

---

## Why Pi 5 is different

The Pi 4 and earlier used the `dwc2` driver with `g_ether` module loaded directly via `cmdline.txt`. **This doesn't work on Pi 5 Bookworm** because:

1. The BCM2712 (Pi 5 SoC) USB-C controller wasn't enabled in firmware until early 2024
2. Bookworm replaced `dhcpcd` with NetworkManager, which ignores `/etc/network/interfaces` by default
3. The Pi 5 requires **libcomposite/ConfigFS** to set up USB gadgets, not the old `modules-load=dwc2,g_ether` approach

The new setup script handles all of this automatically, and works on Pi 4 too.

---

## About the "serial + HTML file" idea

You asked about having the RPi expose itself as both a USB drive (serving an HTML file) and a serial device so the webpage could communicate over serial. This is a genuinely clever concept — here's why it doesn't work on iPad specifically, and what does work instead:

**The limitation:** The [Web Serial API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Serial_API) — which lets a webpage talk to a serial/CDC-ACM device — is **not supported on Safari or iOS**. It's only available in Chrome/Edge on desktop. So even if the RPi exposed both a USB mass storage drive (with the HTML file on it) and a CDC-ACM serial port, the webpage couldn't talk back over serial on the iPad.

**Why USB Ethernet + HTTP is better anyway:**
- HTTP over USB Ethernet gives you full-duplex WebSocket connections — effectively the same as a serial link but faster, bidirectional, and with no browser API restrictions
- The MJPEG video stream, WebSocket audio, and file transfers all ride on the same USB cable
- Safari supports all of this natively

If you were doing this on a **desktop Chrome browser** instead of iPad, the serial approach would actually work and be interesting to explore.

---

## Requirements

| | |
|---|---|
| **Pi model** | Raspberry Pi 4 or **5** (also works on Zero 2 W) |
| **OS** | Raspberry Pi OS **Bookworm** (64-bit recommended) |
| **Firmware** | Up to date — run `sudo rpi-eeprom-update` first |
| **Cable** | USB-C **data** cable (not charge-only) |
| **iPad port** | USB-C iPad → USB-C cable direct; Lightning iPad → Lightning-to-USB3 adapter + USB-C cable |

> ⚠️ **Pi 5 power note**: When plugged into an iPad the Pi 5 draws power from the USB connection. The iPad can supply ~2A over USB-C which is usually enough for light use. Under heavy load (video encoding etc.) consider an external USB-C power supply.

---

## Setup

```bash
# 1. Update firmware first (important for Pi 5!)
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo rpi-eeprom-update -a
sudo reboot

# 2. Copy files to Pi
scp -r rpi-link-v3/ pi@raspberrypi.local:~/

# 3. Run setup
cd ~/rpi-link-v3
sudo bash setup.sh
sudo reboot

# 4. Plug USB-C cable → iPad
# 5. Safari → http://raspberrypi.local
#    Fallback: http://192.168.7.2
```

---

## Uninstall

```bash
cd ~/rpi-link-v3
sudo bash uninstall.sh
sudo reboot
```

This removes all services, scripts, config files, and boot changes.

---

## Troubleshooting

| Problem | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| Gadget shows "not bound" in Stats | Check `sudo journalctl -u rpi-link-gadget` — usually a firmware/UDC issue |
| No IP on iPad | Wait ~30s after plugging in; check `sudo systemctl status dnsmasq` |
| `http://raspberrypi.local` doesn't load | Try `http://192.168.7.2` directly; mDNS can be slow on first connect |
| Stream black/no display | Pi is headless — Xvfb virtual display starts automatically |
| Pi 5 gadget not working | Run `sudo rpi-eeprom-update` then reboot; firmware must be 2024+ |
| Audio not working | Run `pulseaudio --check -v`; try `sudo systemctl restart rpi-link` |

```bash
# Check all services
sudo systemctl status rpi-link-gadget rpi-link rpi-link-xvfb

# Live logs
sudo journalctl -u rpi-link -f
sudo journalctl -u rpi-link-gadget -f

# Verify gadget is bound
cat /sys/kernel/config/usb_gadget/rpi-link/UDC

# Check usb0 has an IP
ip addr show usb0
```

---

## File structure

```
rpi-link-v3/
├── setup.sh              ← one-time root setup (Pi 4 + Pi 5)
├── uninstall.sh          ← remove everything cleanly
├── server.py             ← Flask + WebSocket server
├── static/
│   └── index.html        ← full iPad UI
├── lib/
│   ├── gadget.sh         ← libcomposite gadget setup (runs at boot)
│   └── gadget-teardown.sh← clean gadget removal
└── README.md
```
